description: Documented failure cases of university transport platforms in Saudi Arabia (2023-2026)
disclaimer: All platform and individual names are aliases. Data from anonymized founder interviews, former employee accounts, and public records.

cases.causeDetail: Planned 3 months for university ERP integration. Actual time: 11 months. 70% of funding burned before first client. Team had zero prior education sector experience.
lessons: University ERP integration is not a simple API — plan 8-14 months minimum
Personal relationships with university IT teams are the entry key
SAR 1.2M is insufficient runway — need SAR 2.5M+ minimum
A purely technical team (4 engineers, zero sales/marketing/PR) is not viable

cases.causeDetail: Solo founder spent 10 months building the 'perfect product' in isolation. Never met a potential client. Zero sales in 6 months post-launch.
lessons: Build MVP, find first client before writing code
Spend 25% of time on customer discovery, not 100% on development
Solo founder = no one to challenge decisions or push different direction

cases.causeDetail: Strategy: enter at SAR 99/month (market: SAR 349-749), capture market share, raise prices later. Lost ~SAR 200 per subscription. When attempting to raise to SAR 249, 2 of 3 universities withdrew. B2B clients are not loyal to the cheapest — they're loyal to trust.
lessons: Price wars fail in B2B — relationships and trust matter more than price
Client switching costs (data migration, staff training) exceed price differences
Unit economics must be profitable from day one or have clear 12-18 month path

cases.causeDetail: CTO wanted 'technically advanced architecture.' COO wanted 'rapid commercial launch.' Dispute turned personal. CTO resigned with 2 loyal developers. Product stalled 6 months. 6 of 11 total employees resigned within 3 months.
lessons: Founders' agreement is more important than the product idea
Define final decision authority per domain before founding
Single-person technical dependency = existential risk
Culture cascades — founder disputes poison the entire team

cases.causeDetail: IDOR vulnerability in booking API. Attacker accessed: 700 female students' full names, phone numbers, daily trip schedules, pickup point addresses. University discovered breach from student complaint — not from platform's monitoring. Contract canceled immediately. NCA investigation opened.
lessons: Security is not a feature — it is your license to exist in student transport
RLS, AES-256 encryption, audit log are minimum requirements — not optional
Zero penetration testing before launch = guaranteed failure
In female student transport, a single breach = permanent reputation destruction

cases.causeDetail: Entered during peak competition (2024 — 6 new platforms entered simultaneously). Product was a single feature (driver app) that integrated platforms could easily add for free. Grant-dependent with zero revenue from day one. Regulatory changes added licensing complexity they were unprepared for.
lessons: If your 'product' can be built by a competitor in 2 weeks, you're a feature — not a company
Revenue from day one is critical — grants alone are not a business model
Track regulatory changes — transport licensing requirements evolve

recurringPatterns.description: Platforms planned 2-4 months for university integration; reality was 8-14 months
Funding depleted before reaching first client or break-even. Average time to financial collapse: 14 months
Excellent technical products built in a vacuum — zero client relationships, zero market validation
Founder disputes, single-person dependency, missing complementary skills
